html:
COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) 2026/1404

amending Annexes V and XIV to Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 as regards the entries for the United States of America and Canada in the lists of third countries, territories or zones from which consignments of poultry and poultry breeding material, as well as fresh poultry and game meat, may be imported into the Union.

THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION HAS ADOPTED THIS REGULATION,

having regard to the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union,

having regard to Regulation (EU) 2016/429 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 9 March 2016 on transmissible animal diseases and amending and repealing certain acts in the field of animal health (‘animal health law’) (1), in particular Articles 230.1, 232.1(b) and 232.3(b), and

whereas, pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/429, consignments of animals, breeding material and products of animal origin from third countries or territories may only be imported into the Union if the third countries or territories are listed in accordance with Article 230.1 of that Regulation.

Whereas, Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/692 (2) lays down animal health requirements that must be fulfilled for the import into the Union of consignments of certain species and categories of animals, breeding material and products of animal origin from third countries, territories or zones thereof, or, in the case of aquaculture animals, from establishments thereof.

Whereas, Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 (3) establishes lists of third countries, territories or zones thereof from which the species and categories of animals, breeding material and products of animal origin covered by Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/692 may be imported into the Union. These lists and certain general provisions relating to the lists are set out in Annexes I to XXII to that Implementing Regulation.

Specifically, Appendices V and XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 contain lists of third countries, territories, or zones from which shipments of poultry, poultry breeding material, and fresh meat from poultry and game birds are permitted to be imported into the Union.

On June 10, 2026, the United States notified the Commission of three new outbreaks of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) in poultry in the states of Indiana and New Jersey, which were confirmed on June 3, 2026, and June 8, 2026, through laboratory analysis (RT-PCR).

Following these recent outbreaks of HPAI, the United States' veterinary authorities have announced the establishment of restriction zones of at least 10 km around the affected facilities, and that culling has been carried out to control HPAI and limit the spread of the disease.

The United States has provided the Commission with further information on the epidemiological situation in its territory and on the measures taken to prevent further spread of HPAI as a result of these outbreaks.

The Commission has now evaluated the information provided by the United States. Considering the animal health situation in the areas subject to the restrictions established by the United States' veterinary authorities, the import into the Union of shipments of poultry and poultry breeding material, as well as fresh meat from poultry and game birds, from the areas affected by the new outbreaks of the disease in that third country should be temporarily suspended to protect the animal health status of the Union, taking into account the dates on which the outbreaks were confirmed. Therefore, the entries for the United States in the tables in Part 1, Section B, and in Part 2 of Appendix V, as well as in the table in Part 1, Section B, of Appendix XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404, should be amended accordingly.

Furthermore, Canada has provided the Commission with updated information regarding the epidemiological situation within its territory concerning the previous outbreaks of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), which led to the temporary suspension of imports into the Union of shipments of poultry and poultry breeding material, as well as fresh poultry and game meat, in accordance with the table in Part 1, Section B of Annex V and the table in Part 1, Section B of Annex XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404.

On May 21, 2026, and June 12, 2026, Canada provided updated information to the Commission regarding the animal health situation and the measures taken in response to nine outbreaks of HPAI in poultry in the provinces of Alberta, British Columbia, Manitoba, and Saskatchewan, which were confirmed between October 9, 2025, and April 24, 2026, through laboratory analysis (RT-PCR).

Canada has informed the Commission that, following these previous HPAI outbreaks, it implemented culling measures to control and limit the spread of the disease, and also completed the necessary cleaning and disinfection procedures after the culling at the affected poultry farms.

The Commission has evaluated the information provided by Canada and considers that it provides sufficient guarantees that the animal health situation that led to the temporary suspension of imports into the Union of shipments of poultry and poultry breeding material, as well as fresh poultry and game meat, from the affected zones, in accordance with the table in Part 1, Section B of Annex V and the table in Part 1, Section B of Annex XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404, no longer poses a threat to animal or human health within the Union, and that the import into the Union of such shipments from the affected zones in Canada, from which imports were temporarily suspended, should therefore be allowed again. The entries for Canada in these tables in the annexes should therefore be amended accordingly.

Given the recent outbreaks of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) in the United States, and to avoid unnecessary disruptions to trade with Canada, the amendments made by this regulation to Annexes V and XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 should take effect as soon as possible.

The measures prescribed in this regulation are consistent with the opinion of the Standing Committee on Plants, Animals, Food and Feed.

Annexes V and XIV of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 shall be amended in accordance with the Annex to this regulation.

This regulation shall enter into force on the day following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union.

This regulation is binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States.

(1) OJ L 84, 31.3.2016, p. 1, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2016/429/oj.

(2) Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/692 of 30 January 2020 supplementing Regulation (EU) 2016/429 of the European Parliament and of the Council with regard to rules for the introduction into the Union and the movement and handling after introduction of consignments of certain animals, breeding material and products of animal origin (OJ L 174, 3.6.2020, p. 379, ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg_del/2020/692/oj).

(3) Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/404 of 24 March 2021 establishing lists of third countries, territories or zones from which animals, breeding material and products of animal origin are admitted to the Union.
tldr: The EU is introducing new regulations for the import of poultry meat from the United States and Canada to protect animal health. Imports from certain regions of the United States are being halted due to new disease outbreaks, while imports from certain regions of Canada are now being permitted again.
lattlast:
The European Commission has changed the regulations regarding the amount of poultry and game that can be imported into the EU. This is due to the spread of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), a serious bird flu that affects birds.

Recently, three new outbreaks of bird flu have been detected in the states of Indiana and New Jersey in the United States. To prevent the disease from spreading to Europe, the EU has decided to temporarily halt all imports of poultry, game, and fresh poultry meat from these specific areas.

The situation is different in Canada. There have been previous outbreaks in the provinces of Alberta, British Columbia, Manitoba, and Saskatchewan. Because the Canadian authorities have done a good job in slaughtering infected animals and cleaning the facilities, the EU assesses that the risk is now over.

Therefore, imports of poultry and poultry meat from the previously affected areas in Canada to the EU can now resume. The purpose of the new regulations is to protect animals and people in Europe while also trying to maintain trade with Canada as much as possible.
